flashcards.front: What is memory management in operating systems?
flashcards.back: Memory management is the OS function that controls primary memory allocation to processes, ensuring efficient utilization of RAM while maintaining process isolation and maximizing system performance.

flashcards.front: What is swapping in memory management?
flashcards.back: Swapping is the process of moving entire processes between disk and memory. A process is moved from RAM to disk (swap out) when not in use, and brought back to memory (swap in) when needed.

Internal fragmentation occurs when memory is allocated in fixed-size blocks, leaving unused space within the allocated block. It is the difference between the allocated memory and the actual memory requested by a process.

External fragmentation occurs when free memory is scattered in small blocks throughout RAM, making it impossible to allocate a large contiguous block of memory to a process, even though total free memory is sufficient.

Paging is a memory management scheme that eliminates the need for contiguous allocation by dividing physical memory into fixed-size frames and logical memory into same-sized pages. It uses a page table to map pages to frames.

A page table is a data structure maintained by the OS that stores mappings between virtual page numbers and physical frame numbers. Each entry contains the frame address and control bits like valid/invalid, protection, and dirty bits.

Virtual memory is a technique that allows execution of processes that may not be completely in memory. It provides the illusion of having more memory than physically available by using disk space as an extension of RAM.

Demand paging is a virtual memory management scheme where pages are loaded into memory only when a page fault occurs (i.e., when the process tries to access a page not currently in memory). Initially, no pages are loaded.

A page fault occurs when a process accesses a page that is not currently in physical memory. The OS must then load the required page from secondary storage into an available frame, potentially evicting another page.

flashcards.front: What is the FIFO page replacement algorithm?
flashcards.back: FIFO (First In First Out) replaces the oldest page in memory when a page fault occurs. It maintains a queue of pages in memory order and evicts the page that has been in memory the longest.
LRU (Least Recently Used) replaces the page that has not been accessed for the longest time. It tracks page usage history and evicts the least recently used page on a page fault.

Thrashing occurs when a process spends more time swapping pages in and out of memory than executing, due to excessive page faults. This happens when the degree of multiprogramming is too high or memory is overcommitted.

Segmentation is a memory management technique that divides memory into variable-sized segments based on logical divisions of a program (code, data, stack, heap). Each segment has a base and limit register for protection.

flashcards.front: What is a Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)?
flashcards.back: TLB is a hardware cache that stores recent virtual-to-physical address translations. It is a special, fast-lookup cache that reduces page table access time by keeping frequently used page table entries readily available.

Copy-on-write is an optimization where the parent and child processes initially share the same memory pages. A page is copied only when one process attempts to modify it, saving memory and processing time.
